title: The Essays of Warren Buffett
subtitle: Lessons for Corporate America
author: Warren E. Buffett

editor.name: Lawrence A. Cunningham
editor.role: Professor of Law, Benjamin N. Cardozo School of Law

editionReferenced: 1st Carrum Asset Management edition (paperback, 1998); 2nd paperback edition, 2001
themes: managers are stewards of shareholder capital, not agents of their own enrichment
full and fair disclosure is the foundation of trustworthy corporate governance
price and value diverge frequently: the intelligent investor exploits the gap
owner-founder mentality outperforms the hired-manager model
accounting is a tool for communication, not obfuscation
executive compensation should reward returns to owners, not earnings from retained capital
markets are manic-depressive auction mechanisms, not efficient calculators
long-term compounding of owner earnings beats short-term earnings activity
trust and integrity are durable competitive advantages that cannot be faked
Berkshire's shareholder letters are a model of plain-spoken, high-quality corporate communication
description: Lawrence A. Cunningham curates, annotates, and organises selections from four decades of Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letters into seven thematic sections covering corporate governance, finance, common stock valuation, M&A, and accounting policy. The edition referenced is the original 1998 Carrum Asset Management publication (ISBN 9781576600760, ~350pp); a second Carrum edition followed in 2001. Cunningham's role is editorial and curatorial, not authorial: the value lies in the thematic grouping, editorial annotations, and introduction that transform a chronological archive into a systematic textbook of investment and governance philosophy. Widely cited in both law school curricula and investment education programs.
toc: VII. Accounting Policy and Tax Matters
